ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Kathi Roll; closest defensible match is the generic wrap food product class FOODON_03315091.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Kathi Roll code; A0FJP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible anchor. Preparation context (paratha wrap, butter chicken gravy) documented in notes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps specifically to Chicken Kathi Roll as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Chicken Kathi Roll weighs approximately 300 g and constitutes a complete single-serve meal, comprising a pan-fried paratha wrapped around butter chicken filling with onions and chutneys.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) — Chicken curry and paratha components; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for chicken and wheat flour entries
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Kathi Roll as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by component-weighted estimation: paratha (~40% by weight, ICMR-NIN paratha entry), butter chicken gravy with chicken (~50% by weight, ICMR-NIN chicken curry entry), and onion/chutney (~10% by weight, USDA SR Legacy). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this estimation and have been retained with minor rounding corrections. Energy cross-checked via Atwater factors: protein 11.67 g × 4 + fat 13.33 g × 9 + carbohydrate 13.33 g × 4 = 46.68 + 119.97 + 53.32 = 219.97 kcal ≈ 220 kcal/100g (input reports 216.67 kcal — within acceptable rounding tolerance; retained as-is).

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Chapati/paratha analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for Indian flatbread (chapati, GI ~52–70 range)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Kathi Roll as a composite dish. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a wheat-flour paratha, analogous to chapati/Indian flatbread entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 52–70, mean ~62). The butter chicken filling and cream content slow gastric emptying, moderating the effective GI slightly below plain paratha. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 62 × available carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 13.33 g/100g = 40.0 g) / 100 = 24.8 g, rounded to 24.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g; estimated 900 mg per full 300 g serving — approaching 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a roll (~150 g), request reduced salt in paratha dough and gravy, and avoid high-sodium chutneys to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat-flour paratha (medium-to-high GI ~62) with low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100g) produces a high glycemic load of ~24.9 per serving, elevating postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute whole-wheat paratha to increase fibre, reduce the paratha portion while increasing the chicken filling, avoid sugary chutneys, and monitor postprandial glucose response given the estimated glycemic load of ~25 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g (18.0 g per 300 g serving) from butter and cream in both paratha and butter chicken gravy, combined with cholesterol 83.33 mg/100g (250 mg per serving), exceeds recommended limits for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request preparation with reduced butter and cream, substitute with olive oil or low-fat yogurt where possible, and pair with a high-fibre side to mitigate the saturated fat and cholesterol burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; butter and cream are pro-inflammatory and not covered by the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to its high saturated fat content from butter and cream; substituting with olive oil and low-fat yogurt and retaining anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) and cashews would reduce inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kathi Roll is a popular Indian street food dish widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of a flaky, pan-fried wheat-flour paratha wrap filled with tender chicken cooked in a rich and creamy butter chicken gravy — a tomato-and-cream-based sauce spiced with turmeric, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and chili — along with sliced onions, green chutney, and mint chutney. The paratha is typically prepared with butter or ghee, giving it a layered, slightly crispy texture. The overall flavour profile is rich, mildly spiced, and creamy, with a soft interior and lightly charred exterior from pan-frying. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 650 kcal, 35 g protein, 40 g fat (18 g saturated), and 40 g carbohydrate. The dish is moderate-to-high in sodium (~900 mg per serving) and saturated fat, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The refined wheat paratha contributes a medium glycemic index (~62) and a high glycemic load (~25 per serving), making portion control essential for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Anti-inflammatory ingredients including turmeric, ginger, garlic, and cashews are present in the butter chicken gravy. The dish is halal-suitable and contains gluten, dairy, and tree nut allergens. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is a staple offering at Indian fast-food stalls and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Kathi Roll as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from component-weighted estimation using ICMR-NIN (paratha, chicken curry) and USDA SR Legacy (onion, chutney components), consistent with the input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chapati/Indian flatbread entries. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish (paratha + butter chicken gravy) is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN 2017 or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite preparation is not reported as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy; estimated ~220 mg/100g from tomatoes, onions, chicken, and cashews (used in health context reasoning only, not entered as a per_100g value pending approved-source confirmation). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and proximate values are not available for this composite dish preparation in ICMR-NIN 2017 or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 4. trans_fat_g 0.17 g/100g is retained from input record; this is plausible given butter content in paratha and gravy. 5. Input record listed two portions both labelled with 300 g — the default serving size of 300 g (1 roll) has been adopted. 6. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a delivery/menu platform and is not an approved nutrient source under Project Veritas v2; it has been replaced with ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central as the nutrient evidence basis. 7. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on analogous chapati/Indian flatbread entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving = 62 × 40.0 g available carbohydrate / 100 = 24.8, reported as 24.9.
